title: Chapter 14: First Day
content: The north light hits the long industrial table before I do.

I stand in the doorway of the Ashford Foundation atelier and let it wash over me — that particular milky luminescence that painters and dressmakers have killed for, the kind of light that makes color tell the truth. The ceiling is high and origami-folded with white panels that bounce and soften the gray June morning into something like grace. Six tables run in two rows down the length of the room, each one a broad steel-lipped island with its own task lamp, its own bolt rack, its own silence waiting to be broken.
But it's the wall that stops me.

Floor to ceiling, maybe forty feet across, organized by color family in columns that bleed into each other like a spectrum having a long slow orgasm. Silk samples. Wool. Organza. Charmeuse. Douppioni. Velvet in three weights. Cotton lawn so fine it breathes. Linen the color of old bone. I walk toward it the way you walk toward a body of water after days in the desert — helplessly, already reaching.

My fingers find a swatch of slate blue wool. Soft, toothy, with a body-memory of drape I haven't felt since — no. I pull my hand back. The fabric is dusty. Some of these swatches haven't been touched in decades. I can smell it: wool and silk and the faint sweet rot of old cellulose fiber, layered under the chemical kiss of sizing, under the ghost of someone else's perfume from 1987 or 1952 or whenever the last person who knew what this wall was for stood exactly where I'm standing.
They've been waiting.

I set my bag on the third table from the window — the one with the best light, the one nobody else chose, which tells me something about how my arrival was announced or, more likely, not announced. I unroll my tool kit. The shears first: eight-inch Gingher dressmaker shears, heavy and balanced, the edges still sharp after four years of use. I lay them parallel to the table's edge. Chalk — white and blue, twin wedges in a tin. The tin used to hold Altoids; now it holds my history in compressed calcium carbonate. Pins. Glass-head pins in a ceramic dish I bought at a flea market in Chelsea, because even immortals need small aesthetic mercies.
And the thimble.

I pick it up. It's light — lighter than it looks, because it's silver, not steel, and the silver has worn thin at the tip from three centuries of pushing needles through fabric that didn't want to be pierced. The surface is chased with a pattern of acanthus leaves, worn almost smooth. I can feel the ghost-grooves under my thumb. Venice, 1743. A silversmith near the Rialto who owed Marco a favor and made this for me in an afternoon, laughing at the woman who wanted a thimble chased like a doge's palazzo.
I set it on the table. It makes a tiny sound — a *clink* that is also a confession, if anyone's listening.
My space now.
It feels like a trap I chose to walk into.

The atelier holds six designers, including me. I learn this by counting the occupied tables as the room fills around me. They arrive one by one, carrying coffee cups and fabric rolls and the particular self-seriousness of people who've been told they're the best until they believe it. And they look at me. Not the quick-glance kind of looking — the slow, appraising kind. The kind that measures and categorizes and decides where to file you in the hierarchy of threat.

I don't have a portfolio. I don't have a degree from Central Saint Martins or Parsons or Antwerp. I don't have an Instagram grid full of editorial spreads or a last name that opens atelier doors. I have a tool kit and a wall of fabric and the memory of fitting gowns on duchesses who've been dead for three hundred years, and none of that goes on a résumé.

The woman at the nearest table — dark hair, sharp cheekbones, the kind of posture that comes from ballet or rigor or both — glances at my setup. Her eyes snag on the shears. Then the thimble. She looks away, but I can see her recalculating.

The second designer, a man with round glasses and paint-stained fingers, doesn't even bother to hide his curiosity. He openly stares until the dark-haired woman catches his eye and shakes her head slightly. *Don't engage.*
Fine by me. I'm not here to make friends.

I sip the coffee I poured from the atelier's communal pot. It's bitter, over-extracted, burnt at the bottom in a way that coats the tongue with a char that no amount of sugar fixes. It tastes like institutional optimism, like someone trying very hard and missing the mark by exactly enough to remind you this is a workplace and not a dream.
I'm on my second sip when Maren Voss materializes beside me.

She moves like someone who learned early that noise is a form of weakness. One moment the space to my left is empty; the next, she's there, close enough that I can smell her perfume — something green and cold, vetiver over mineral, the kind of scent that costs more than most people's rent.

"How lovely to have you." Her voice is silk over glass. The silk is the warmth. The glass is what cuts. She tilts her head, studying me the way a jeweler studies a stone they haven't decided is real yet. "I hear you're something of a... mystery."
I set down the coffee. The mug clicks against the steel table. "I'm a designer, Ms. Voss. Nothing mysterious about that."

Her smile spreads — too wide, too warm, the kind of warmth that's actually a temperature reading. She's measuring how I respond to being seen. "Of course." A beat. "I look forward to seeing your work."
Her eyes move. Not to me — to my tools. The shears. The chalk. And then the thimble.

Her gaze lands on it the way a hand lands on a bannister: steadying, deliberate, not quite necessary but impossible to resist. I watch her notice the patina. The acanthus chasing, worn smooth. The shape of something that has no business being on a twenty-first-century worktable unless it came from an estate sale or a museum gift shop — and this thimble came from neither.
Her eyes linger.
My stomach drops.

She looks up, meets my gaze, and the smile hasn't changed by a single millimeter. "Welcome to the atelier," she says, and then she's gone — moving on to the next designer, the next table, the next piece of information to catalog and store.
I pick up the thimble. Turn it over in my fingers. Consider putting it away.
I don't. I slide it onto my ring finger, where it belongs. Where it's belonged since the Rialto.
There's a moment — it happens without permission, without announcement — where the accumulated pressure of four centuries simply... releases.
I pick up the chalk and I start to draw.

The first sketch comes out like blood from a wound: fast, hot, and unstoppable. A dress. The bodice is structured, boned, with a dropped shoulder seam that references 1623 Flemish mourning wear — I can still see the widows in the Rijnsburg church, their black wool like architecture, the way grief made armor out of fabric. But the skirt is different. I draw it in a bias cut that wouldn't exist for another three hundred years, the silk falling like water from the hip, the hemline asymmetrical, jagged, as if the mourning has started to come apart at the edges.

Chalk on paper: a soft, grinding whisper, like a small animal moving through dry leaves. I can hear it under the ambient hum of the atelier, under the murmur of other designers conferring in low voices. My chalk. My sound.
I flip the page. Start again.

A coat. The silhouette is pure 1743 Venetian masquerade — the exaggerated shoulders, the nipped waist, the flamboyant sleeve that bellows at the elbow before tightening at the wrist. But I strip the embellishment. No embroidery, no gold thread, no pomposity. Just the architecture of the thing, rendered in matte black wool with a single line of exposed red stitching at the collar, like a wound that's been sutured but not hidden.
Flip. Start again.

A suit. The jacket borrows from 1867 — the gothic tailoring, the elongated silhouette, the romantic morbidity of a shape that suggests the body inside might not be entirely alive. But the trousers are 2026: cropped, raw-hemmed, worn over boots that look like they could kick through a door. The collision should be ugly. It isn't. On paper, it's a conversation across time, and both parties are furious and articulate and refusing to compromise.
The chalk is a stump now. I grab the blue one.

I'm aware, in the periphery, that the murmuring has stopped. The other designers are looking. First one, then another — drawn the way moths are drawn, the way anyone with eyes is drawn to something that shouldn't exist. I can feel their gazes land on my sketches and slide off, unsettled. They expected fraud. They found velocity. The suspicion on their faces is mutating into something less comfortable: the recognition of talent they can't match, can't even account for, because it operates on a timeline they don't have access to.
I should slow down. I should mask. I should give them the thirty-two-year-old's portfolio they expect — good, careful, derivative in the way that passes for sophistication.
I don't slow down.
I draw.
The fourth sketch.
I don't choose it. It chooses me.

My hand moves to the center of a fresh sheet and I feel the shape arrive before I see it — a silhouette I know the way I know my own skeleton, the way I know the sound of my name in a voice that no longer exists. The dress.

I draw the neckline first. A wide boat collar that sits just off the shoulder, exposing the clavicle, the soft hollow of the throat where Marco used to press his lips and whisper that I tasted like the sea. The line of the collar is exact — I've drawn it a hundred times, a thousand, in margins and on tablecloths and once, in a hotel room in Lisbon, on my own thigh with a pen that ran out before I could finish the sleeve.

I draw the sleeve. Three-quarter length, fitted to the elbow, then released into a gentle flare that would move like smoke when the wearer reached for a glass, for a hand, for the last thing they'd ever reach for. I shade the fabric with the flat of the chalk — not black, not navy, but midnight blue. The exact shade the Venetian sky turned at 11:47 on the night of October 18th, 1743, when Marco's breath caught once, twice, and then stopped, and I held his hand and watched the color drain from his face while the color of the sky outside the window did the opposite — deepened, saturated, as if the universe were drinking him in through the glass.
My hands are shaking.

I don't stop. I can't. The waist is empire, cut just below the bust, releasing into a skirt that falls in heavy pleats to the floor. I know how this fabric would move. I know how much of it I'd need — six meters, seven if I'm being generous with the train. I know the thread count, the weight, the way it would sound when the wearer turned in a quiet room. I have known these things for three hundred years.
I'm not designing.
I'm mourning. On paper. In a room full of witnesses.
The chalk snaps.

I look down. The sketch is finished — or as finished as it will ever be, because the dress itself has never been finished, will never be finished, because the person it was for has been dead for three centuries and I am still drawing it from memory in an atelier in New York City on my first day of a job I took because I'm pretending to be someone who doesn't carry a dead man's last century in her hands.

I set the broken chalk down. My fingers are white with dust. Across the room, someone clears their throat. The silence is thick, specific. The other designers aren't looking at me anymore — they're looking at the sketches on my table, and their faces have moved past unease into something that looks, uncomfortably, like awe. Which is worse. Suspicion, I can work with. Awe is a spotlight I can't survive.
I slide the 1743 sketch under the others. Face down. As if that could hide what it is.
My phone vibrates in my bag. The sound is obscene in the atelier's hush — a small, tinny intrusion that makes the dark-haired woman at the nearest table flinch.
I dig it out. Rowan's name on the screen.
I step into the hallway. The noise out here is different — building sounds, HVAC thrum, the muffled percussion of heels on marble floors. Normal sounds. Safe sounds.
"Hey." His voice is warm, the way a kitchen is warm when someone's been cooking in it. It arrives in my ear like an antidote. "How's the first day?"
I lean against the wall. The plaster is cool through my shirt. I close my eyes.

In my mind, I can still see the sketch. The midnight blue. The boat collar. The sleeve that flares at the elbow like smoke, like a question, like the last gesture of a hand that was reaching for something it would never touch.
Three hundred years. I've been trying to make this dress for three hundred years.
"It's going well," I say.
Rowan laughs softly. "Yeah? You sound like you've already terrified everyone."
"Maybe a little."
"That's my girl." A pause. The warmth doesn't waver. "I'll let you get back to it. Dinner tonight?"
"Dinner," I echo. "Yes."
I hang up. Stand in the hallway. Breathe.

Maren noticed the thimble. She filed it without comment — a small silver artifact on a designer's table, too old, too worn, too specific. The other designers noticed the speed. The way the sketches came out like breath, like blood, like four centuries of compressed vision finally given room. And now there's a sketch on my table — a 1743 silhouette drawn from memory, with the kind of period-precise detail that no thirty-two-year-old designer should possess.
The evidence is accumulating.

I push off the wall and walk back into the atelier. The north light has shifted. The room is brighter now, less forgiving. Every surface is a mirror reflecting a version of me I haven't learned to hide yet.
I sit down. I pick up the unbroken chalk.
I keep working. What else is there to do?
